data.title: Philippines ‘comfort women’ demand apology during Japan foreign minister visit
data.description:
An advocacy group representing Filipino victims of wartime sexual slavery by Japanese soldiers will renew its decades-old demand for a formal state apology and reparations as Japan’s foreign minister visits the Philippines this week.
The group, Lila Pilipina (League of Filipino Grandmothers), said it plans to press its appeal during the visit of Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi, who is set to arrive on Thursday.
“We demand a formal public apology and reparation based on the apology,” said Sharon Cabusao-Silva, a spokeswoman for the group.
However, analysts say Japan’s long-held position makes new reparations unlikely.

Tokyo maintains that all wartime claims were settled through post-war treaties, including the Philippines-Japan Reparations Agreement of 1956, and that Manila has waived all further claims on behalf of its citizens.
In the 1990s, Japan established the Asian Women’s Fund to provide “atonement payments” funded by private donations to “comfort women”, as the sex slaves were known, rather than from state coffers.
Many Filipino survivors rejected the payments, with advocates arguing the initiative lacked official acknowledgement of Japan’s legal responsibility.
“Even by the Japanese government’s own admission, it was not the official compensation,” Silva said. “The funds came from Japanese citizens and private corporations.”
However, she acknowledged that some members of her group had accepted payments from the fund out of economic necessity.
Of the more than 170 “comfort women” that Lila Pilipina has documented, only six are still alive.

A closed case?
The Philippine Department of Foreign Affairs has not confirmed whether the issue will be raised in Motegi’s talks with Foreign Secretary Theresa Lazaro.
The officials are expected to focus on regional security and Japan’s expanding role as a development and defence partner during their talks.
Edmund Tayao, a law professor at the San Beda Graduate School of Law in Manila, said the Philippine government would not prevent “any group” from raising the issue of reparations. “But they will also not support it,” he said.
Japan is the Philippines’ largest source of official development help, providing about US$13.23 billion in 2024 – roughly one-third of the total.
These funds have been used to finance flagship infrastructure projects, including the Metro Manila Subway, North-South Commuter Railway and Davao City Bypass.
Their policy is simply to ignore it
Sharon Cabusao-Silva, Lila Pilipina spokeswoman
Silva said this financial support had dissuaded Philippine governments “past and present” from raising the reparations issue with Japan.
“They cannot be relied upon to take up the case,” she said. “their policy is simply to ignore it.”
Legal experts note that Tokyo considers the matter closed and is unlikely to want to revisit it. “For the Japanese, that has always been a sensitive subject,” Tayao said.
The Philippine government had also waived its right to raise the issue, said Aldrin Alba of the Political Economic Elemental Researchers and Strategists think tank.
However, Alba noted that a United Nations women’s rights committee had previously ruled the Philippines violated victims’ rights by failing to provide reparations or recognition. Survivors of wartime sexual slavery could seek to enforce that decision in Philippine courts, he said.

Diane Desierto, a Filipino legal professor who served as co-counsel in that ruling, said there was “hesitation” in applying the ruling as it could open the “floodgates of litigation” for other wartime-era claims “long deemed settled”.
Japan’s stance on the issue has wavered over the decades. Initial apologies in the 1990s gave way to denials under former prime minister Shinzo Abe, who maintained that there was no proof of sexual coercion.
More recently, a Japanese diplomat visiting Manila in 2024 expressed “deep remorse” for the suffering inflicted on Filipino women during the war.
“The dignity and honour of those former comfort women was injured,” Japanese foreign ministry official Kaneko Mariko reportedly said in an interview.
“The government of Japan has been extending, expressing apologies and deep remorse for those actions during the war.”

data.title: LIV Golf’s Majestiks optimistic new look all-British line-up can benefit from rule changes
data.description:
With a new look for a new season and a LIV Golf with new rules, Majesticks GC co-captains Lee Westwood and Ian Poulter see a year of potential after the struggles of past campaigns.
An off-season when Laurie Canter opted to join as a replacement for Henrik Stenson rather than head to the PGA Tour, and saw Sam Horsfield return for another year, has given Majesticks an all-British line-up for the first time.
Coupled with the move to four round tournaments rather than the three that was one of LIV’s unique selling points, it is a quartet Poulter believes should have the group moving “up the team order like I feel we probably should”.
In truth, they could not do much worse. Majesticks finished 11th out of 13 teams last year and ninth the year before that, when they finished fourth in the stops in Hong Kong, Nashville and Chicago.
They ended the 2023 season, LIV’s first full year, 11th out of 12 teams and the second place in Bedminster in 2022 is still the only time they have made the podium.
Four rounds are hardly a guarantee things will improve, but Poulter points to performances on the final Sundays across 2025 as evidence that in several cases Majesticks were not as far behind as results might suggest.

“You know, last year and even the year before, we played well on Sunday, which means we were trending in the right direction and [then] the tournament finishes,” Poulter told the Post during LIV media week in Florida.
“You want to get off to that fast start in every single tournament you play, but three rounds seems like the tournament’s over when you just got going. So I’m hopeful that it’s good for Majesticks.”
They will all need to be on their games as individuals too, with relegation expanded to 20 per cent of the 57-man field, up from the six players, including Stenson, who lost their spots last year.
The Swede fell victim to a Poulter comeback for the ages at the event in Indianapolis, with the Englishman rattling off four birdies in his closing four holes to survive for another year.
Poulter welcomed the relegation change, believing it would “inevitably” lead to the awarding of world ranking points for LIV tournaments.
“It’s a shame we haven’t had them already or the news that we’re going to get them,” he said. “But look, it’s inevitable at some stage that they’re going to come.
“The fact 20 per cent of the guys will change at the end of the year and fall into the relegation zone means there is a pathway to bringing people back into the league.
“It’s hard for those people, and I’ve been close to that last year and managed to scrape out. So, there’ll be a few in that position and hopefully I’m not one of those.”
Despite the optimism, Westwood said they had yet to set any goals for the season, which begins next month in Riyadh.
The tour then heads to Adelaide and arrives in Hong Kong for the third time in the first week of March.
“We haven’t been particularly successful over the last couple of years, so we’re obviously looking to improve on those results and get podiums,” Westwood said. “But you’ve just got to start at the grass roots of it all. Start playing some good golf as a team, and shooting some low scores. And that [hopefully] snowballs into good results.”

Hong Kong is almost home away from home for Majesticks, who, among others, are sponsored by HSBC and sportswear brand Castore, which also provides the city’s rugby team with their kit.
The team’s rebrand, complete with British bulldog logo and a red, white and blue colour scheme, follows a theme running through the league over the past few weeks.
Stinger, a South African team, has evolved into Southern Guards – Rhino included – Iron Heads, minus the departed Kevin Na, have become Korean Golf Club, while Sergio Garcia has an entirely Spanish group of Fireballs for the first time.
When the league launched in 2022, Ollie Banks, one of the Majesticks’ co-team principals, said LIV “didn’t really want to geopin a lot of these teams”.
But it’s been one of CEO Scott O’Neil’s priorities since taking over last year, and “what’s happened is organically it’s kind of taken shape”.

data.title: Fiery Sichuan, authentic seafood: favourite Hong Kong restaurants of banker turned teacher
data.description:
Former banker turned art teacher Ernesto Ng will present a calligraphy exhibition, “Chill With Ink”, with his students and photographer Michael Lee at the Hong Kong Arts Centre from January 16 to 19. He spoke to Andrew Sun.
I am a retired banker but my passions are meals with good friends, calligraphy and being involved with Xu Shan, a charity promoting Chinese intangible culture.
In terms of cuisine, I prefer Chinese and Japanese. Cantonese-style seafood is the best because chefs know how to bring out the fresh taste of the ingredients.
I’m a member of several private members’ clubs, so I often eat there, but I also enjoy a bowl of curry braised beef brisket rice and coconut red bean shaved ice at any Hong Kong-style cafe.

Six Furlong (4/F, Happy Valley Clubhouse, Shan Kwong Road, Happy Valley. Tel: 2966 1360) at the Hong Kong Jockey Club is like a second home. I really like the pork chop baked rice and mandarin fish soup with rice noodles.
The Hongkong Japanese Club (5/F, Tower 535, 535 Jaffe Road, Causeway Bay. Tel: 2577 5796) is also an old favourite. I like their beef rice bowl and sukiyaki.
The owner of the Sichuan restaurant Chuan Yue Shi Kong (Room C, 1/F, Howard Factory Building, 66 Tsun Yip Street, Kwun Tong. Tel: 2988 8886) is an old friend. I always order the sour fish soup.

I enjoy Northern Chinese cuisine with all the noodles and hearty meat dishes, which go well with Mao-tai. I do a lot of entertaining in Zhejiang Heen (1/F-3/F, ZJ 300, 300-306 Lockhart Road, Wan Chai. Tel: 2877 9011). I know the chefs well and I can order dishes not on the menu.
WIN 8 Fusion Chinese Restaurant (2/F, Winland Square, 318 Hennessy Road, Wan Chai. Tel: 2121 5188) is a hidden gem.. There are two big private rooms with karaoke where you can party past midnight.

I enjoy introducing Hong Kong’s authentic cuisine, especially seafood, to visiting friends. In Sai Kung, I like Tung Kee Seafood Restaurant (Shop 11-15, Siu Yat House, Hoi Pong Square, Sai Kung. Tel: 2791 9886) for the salt and pepper mantis shrimp.
In Lei Yue Mun, we go to Lei Yue Mun Seafood Restaurant (G/F, 26-30 Hoi Pong Road West, Lei Yue Mun. Tel: 2727 2820), which is perfect for a large group.
I have done a lot of food trips with my art group, too. A Shanghai trip was recently planned around hairy crab season. For years I have been going to Xie Zhen Xiang (1/F, The Bund 27, The Roosevelt Building, 27 Zhongshan East 1st Road, Huangpu district, Shanghai. Tel: +86 193 0119 3710) for the crab noodle set.
Le Patio La Famille (JuFu Mansion, 2/F, JuLu Road 768, Jingan district, Shanghai. Tel: +86 21 6247 7677) was the group favourite for the trip. Every single dish was great, including the best stinky tofu. Their chicken in claypot dish was also excellent.

data.title: Why the US may find Iran’s air defence system harder to break than Venezuela’s
data.description:
A bit more than a week after the United States abducted former Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro in a raid on Caracas, US President Donald Trump is threatening military action against Iran, which is seeing a massive wave of anti-government protests.
Yet unlike Venezuela, a strike aimed at taking out the Iranian leadership could prove harder, given that Iran’s more sophisticated air defence system is also reported to be integrating China’s most advanced exported technology.
On Tuesday, Trump wrote on his social media platform that Iranian authorities would “pay a big price” for the killing of civilians and urged people in the country to “keep protesting”, adding that “help is on its way”.
The protests in Iran – deemed the largest since the 1979 Islamic Revolution – have been intensifying in the past week with the reported death toll ranging between 2,000 and 12,000.
One of the key moments that determined Maduro’s fate was the failure of the Venezuelan air defence system, which consisted of Russian-made long-range S-300VM and medium-range Buk-M2E surface-to-air missile systems.


According to US media reports, these systems were neutralised by US strikes and electronic warfare using EA-18G Growler aircraft.
The reports also suggested that Venezuela’s air defence radars were not networked and may not have been operational for years – after decades of corruption in Caracas and a struggle to maintain them as Moscow reprioritised the supply of components for its war in Ukraine.
The incompetence of the Venezuelan air defence capability is considered to be one of the critical elements that lubricated US special forces’ capture of Maduro only a few hours into the mission.
In contrast, Iran’s air defence network is considered to be relatively more robust, with not only Russian but also Chinese and locally developed systems. While Iran also operates Russia’s S-300, in the past year Tehran has instituted a major technological reset.
Media reports in September quoting Abolfazl Zohrevand, a member of the Iranian parliament’s National Security Committee, suggested that Iran received Chinese-made HQ-9B surface-to-air missiles from China as well as Russia’s more advanced S-400 in July.
“Russian MiG-29 fighter jets have arrived in Iran and are stationed in Shiraz, while Sukhoi Su-35 jets are also on the way,” Zohrevand said at the time, adding that China’s HQ-9 and Russia’s S-400 systems were being supplied to Iran “in significant numbers”.
Some of the US media reports suggested that Beijing’s supply of the HQ-9B followed an “oil-to-weapons” deal with Tehran, with Iranian crude oil shipments exchanged for advanced Chinese surface-to-air missile systems.
Iran’s recent build-up of its air defence systems came after its failure to defend itself effectively in June from repeated air strikes, which saw Israel’s 12-day air campaign using F-35 fighters and US deployment of strategic bombers and nuclear attack submarines.
The attacks destroyed Iranian nuclear sites as well as the country’s missile bases, radar installations and command structures.




The HQ-9B, part of China’s HQ-9 series of long-range surface-to-air missiles, is likely to be a key component of Iran’s response if the US goes ahead with possible air strikes against the country, along with the new S-400.
With an operational range of 300km (186 miles) and a flight ceiling of 50km (30 miles), the HQ-9B travels with solid-fuel propellant to a speed of more than Mach 4. Its semiactive radar homing guidance system has a separate radar that beams a signal at the target, generating echoes to guide the missiles.
The People’s Liberation Army is known to operate about 300 HQ-9 units, which are also known to have been exported to Morocco, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Pakistan, Egypt and Azerbaijan, as well as Iran.
However, while the HQ-9B is considered the most advanced Chinese air defence export, it has relatively limited combat experience compared with Russia’s S-300 and S-400 systems deployed during its invasion of Ukraine.
Tehran was reported to have conducted the first operational test of the S-400 after its delivery in mid-2025. The Russian system also has a longer operational range of up to 400km than the HQ-9B.
Iran has also reduced its reliance on foreign components by developing indigenous air defence systems, such as the long-range Bavar-373, described by Tehran as a competitor to the S-300, recently upgraded to achieve an operational range of more than 300km.
While its actual combat capabilities are unknown, Iran’s hybrid deployment of various air defence systems suggests more layered networks and fewer vulnerabilities to foreign arms imports than the Venezuelan experience.
Iran, with its indigenous defence industrial base and advanced systems from China and Russia, could create greater complications for any planned US air strikes.

data.title: Sovereign investors cut mainland China exposure as political sensitivities rise
data.description:
Sovereign investors slashed their exposure to mainland China in 2025 amid rising political sensitivities, but experts said selective opportunities and closer bilateral ties could support the outlook.
The world’s second-largest economy received US$4.3 billion in investments from sovereign wealth funds, public pension funds and central banks, down 58 per cent from US$10.3 billion in 2024, according to Global SWF, which tracks 792 such state-owned investors.
“Sovereign investment activity has become increasingly politicised, which has forced some Western state-owned investors such as Canadian and European funds to reduce exposures and avoid major deals in China,” Diego Lopez, founder and managing director of the data platform, told the Post.
Diversification away from developed markets had propped up ventures into China in 2024, but an absence of major deals in 2025 disrupted the goal, Lopez said. He added that the drop was surprising, as Chinese sovereign wealth funds continued to grow in size and cement relationships with their counterparts in the Gulf.
In 2025, the biggest inflows came from Singapore’s Temasek, with US$1.3 billion, followed by Abu Dhabi’s Mubadala Investment and the Qatar Investment Authority, according to Lopez.
“Tech geopolitics is a major factor for the 2025 drop in China’s sovereign deal volume from sovereign wealth funds and other state investors,” said Winston Ma, an adjunct professor at New York University School of Law and former head of North America for China Investment Corporation, the country’s sovereign wealth fund.
“As government-affiliated investors, the sovereign wealth funds were inevitably cautious about geopolitical risks, at a time when some Chinese tech start-ups were already on the US sanctions list in their early years,” he said.

Middle Eastern sovereign investors were more willing to invest in Chinese application-layer artificial intelligence companies than in “hard foundation tech” based on the recent wave of initial public offerings (IPOs) in Hong Kong, Ma added. These hard-tech models and chips carried the highest “export-control and national security sensitivities”, he said.
Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA) invested around US$65 million in the Hong Kong IPO of MiniMax Group, which offers products including an AI agent, audio generation tool MiniMax Audio, multimodal entertainment platform Talkie and visual generation platform Hailuo.
MiniMax’s Chinese rival, Zhipu AI, drew mostly China-focused funds as cornerstone investors in its IPO. Zhipu, founded by professors from China’s Tsinghua University, is backed by and serves mainly domestic institutions. Zhipu and Biren Technology, a Chinese high-performance graphics processing unit designer that listed in Hong Kong earlier this month, are on Washington’s trade blacklist.
AI applications, industrial software and broader digital economy themes were the leading opportunities for global sovereign wealth funds seeking China exposure while aligning with structural digital transformation in their own economies, said Ma.
“China presents significant opportunities in sectors associated with mega trends, including technology, consumer and healthcare,” Lopez said. “We also see Middle East investors increasing collaboration with Chinese counterparts, including Chinese asset managers that have established offices in the Gulf Cooperation Council, which will cement a more consistent deal flow in the years to come.”
Last week, ADIA invested an undisclosed amount in a continuation fund managed by Chinese alternative asset manager CDH Investments. It acted as lead investor in the fund, which was valued at US$770 million at the end of 2024 with a diversified portfolio of mid-to-large-cap assets primarily exposed to China, according to a press release.
“This transaction underscores our continued interest in investing in high-quality assets in China, and demonstrates our ability to invest flexibly and via different deal structures alongside proven partners,” said Hamad Shahwan Aldhaheri, executive director of the private equities department at ADIA.
Last August, ADIA agreed to invest up to US$1.5 billion in GLP, a major logistics and data infrastructure firm with significant operations in China.
China’s real assets, including real estate and infrastructure, attracted the most capital from state-owned investors in 2025, according to Lopez.

data.title: Meet Catherine Missal, whose Tell Me Lies character Bree has a toxic love life
data.description:
Based on Carola Lovering’s 2018 novel of the same name, Hulu’s dual timeline drama Tell Me Lies has finally returned to our screens with a third instalment on January 13.
The series takes place in 2015 with flashbacks to 2007 following the complicated and sometimes toxic love story between Lucy Albright (Grace Van Patten) and Stephen DeMarco (Jackson White) over the course of eight years after meeting in college.
“I’m scared for the audience,” said Van Patten about the new season in an interview with People last November. “Everything’s coming to a head, and everything’s getting more and more tangled and intertwined.”

And while Van Patten and White may play the lead characters in the show, they’re not the only characters with a turbulent love life. At the end of season two, we see one of Lucy’s friends, Bree, played by actress Catherine Missal, navigate a toxic love life of her own after starting and eventually ending an inappropriate relationship with her married college professor. Keeping in mind that this is a dual timeline series, we see Bree getting ready to walk down the aisle on her wedding day in 2015 – only to find out that her soon-to-be husband actually cheated on her with Lucy.
Here’s everything you need to know about Catherine Missal.
She has been acting since she was 10

Missal, 26, kick-started her professional career when she was just 10 years old, starring in the 2009 short film Shopping. She went on to land roles in TV shows like Law & Order: Special Victims Unit and films like the 2025 drama thriller A House of Dynamite starring Idris Elba and Rebecca Ferguson.
Missal’s role in Tell Me Lies is her biggest yet and came after a four-year hiatus between acting roles.
Her older sister is an actress

Missal is not the only one with the acting bug in her family. The actress’ older sister Kelley Missal is known for her role in ABC’s long-running soap opera One Life to Live. She was nominated for a Daytime Emmy for Outstanding Younger Actress in a Drama Series in 2014 for her role in the series.
Like her sister, Kelley has also been acting since she was young. Some of her first appearances were on Saturday Night Live and Comedy Central promos.
Her words of advice to fans

In an interview with The Hollywood Reporter in October 2024, Missal spoke about her role on the show, admitting to the toxic dynamic between the characters. Her word of advice to fans? “If any of your friends in your real life are acting like any of these characters on this show, run away as fast as possible.”

data.title: China set to double down on property market stability
data.description:
For years, Beijing attempted to trade flats for microchips, a bold effort to rewire the economy. But 2025 showed that a hi-tech superstructure cannot be built on the crumbling foundation of a middle-class balance sheet. Now, in the opening 2026 issue of Qiushi, the Communist Party’s most influential journal, a new signal has emerged, indicating that the leadership is prepared to halt the decline.
A key commentary in the journal presents a notable analytical shift. It reaffirms real estate as a foundational industry of the national economy and a primary store of household wealth, while emphasising its significant characteristics as a financial asset. Beyond acknowledging the fundamental changes in supply and demand, the article concedes that restoring confidence will be a gradual process. Consequently, it advocates all-out policy implementation, rejecting the piecemeal approaches of the past to stabilise expectations.
This marks a strategic departure from the rhetoric of the last five years. It suggests the policy pendulum, which had swung decisively towards cost containment, is recalibrating towards asset stability.
To understand this shift, one must recognise the two rival schools of thought that have shaped Chinese macroeconomic policy.
The asset school treats real estate as the economy’s primary credit multiplier. In this view, rising valuations create a wealth effect for families and a high-octane fiscal engine for local governments. This camp dominated the decade leading up to 2015, particularly after the declaration of property as a pillar industry in 2003.
Conversely, the cost school, which gained the upper hand in 2016, viewed property as a structural impediment to productivity. By advocating the principle that houses are for shelter rather than speculation, it sought to lower labour costs and redirect capital towards innovation. Its logic culminated in the “three red lines” policy of 2020, which sought to systematically moderate the financial intensity of real estate.


The Qiushi article arrives as the cost school experiment faces a moment of empirical reckoning. The hope was that cheaper housing would free up consumer spending. Instead, it triggered a cautious hunkering down as household balance sheets began to shrink.
With property values declining substantially from their 2021 peaks, the negative wealth effect has moved from economic theory to the shop floor. High-end restaurants in Beijing and Shanghai are seeing shorter queues, and Ikea has trimmed its footprint. Starbucks, a near 30-year fixture of the Chinese middle class, sold a 60 per cent stake in its China operations to Boyu Capital – a development that suggests even the most resilient consumer brands are adjusting to a leaner reality.
The theory that cheaper houses lead to more spending has encountered a crisis of faith. When around 70 per cent of a family’s wealth is tied to their home, a significant price decline feels like a reduction in life choices, not a discount on living. This psychological headwind continues to pose an obstacle to the consumption-led recovery Beijing is pursuing.
This downturn has created a dual challenge for stability, one that hits both the kitchen table and the local treasury.
Local governments are feeling a historic fiscal squeeze. Land sale revenues, which once provided about half of local budgets, have shrivelled to less than a quarter. While the growth in hi-tech manufacturing is impressive, it offers a different kind of tax base. Semiconductors and electric vehicles generate steady revenue over time, but they cannot replace the massive, upfront capital infusions that land auctions once provided.


At the household level, the notable decline in real estate asset values has produced a shortfall that cannot be offset by income growth alone. For the average citizen, a salary increase would be welcome but seems insignificant amid the depreciation of their principal asset.
The Qiushi article represents a significant evolution in the narrative. It implies that curbing property to stimulate consumption has encountered practical limits. Stabilising the asset has now apparently been recognised as the prerequisite for restoring consumer confidence. It is a vital acknowledgement that hi-tech growth and a stable property market are not rivals, but partners. In short, while hi-tech is the engine of the future, property remains the bedrock of the present.
For global investors, the most critical takeaway is the call for decisive and comprehensive action. This suggests an end to the incremental “drip feed” approach that has characterised recent years. Beijing seems to realise that piecemeal measures only encourage the market to wait and see. To restore faith, the next move needs to be a big bang.


Clearly, one article alone does not complete the task. A full consensus at the highest levels of the party may still be in its formative stages. However, this is the first instance in years that a key party organ has published such a focused defence of the property sector’s systemic importance.
With export markets facing renewed geopolitical headwinds, Beijing is prioritising a robust internal market as its most reliable anchor. This embrace of asset realism marks a commitment to stabilising the national balance sheet.
The real test comes in March at the “two sessions”, China’s annual parliamentary meetings. If the theoretical shift in Qiushi translates into a high-magnitude stimulus package, 2026 may be remembered as the year the floor finally held. We may not be returning to the era of hyper-growth, but we may well be witnessing the start of a more pragmatic, grounded recovery. The question remains: has the floor finally been laid, or is the foundation itself still being rebuilt?

data.title: China’s valuation-driven stock rally on AI optimism to stretch fundraising frenzy in 2026
data.description:
The valuation expansion that elevated Chinese stocks to world-leading gains last year will continue to galvanize equity financing by artificial intelligence companies in 2026 after a slew of eye-popping listings by key players, according to analysts.
More AI players will capitalise on the rich valuations of mainland China’s technology boards to raise funds to drive research and expansion, as China and the US compete for dominance in the world’s cutting-edge technologies.
After a solid run in 2025, tech companies on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges now trade at nearly 40 per cent premiums to those on the Nasdaq 100, boosting the appeal of going public on the home markets.
“I would say capital markets so far are still supportive, whether it’s the global market or the Taiwan market,” said Randy Abrams, a technology analyst at UBS Group, in an interview on Wednesday in Shanghai. “You see the valuations for AI technology, whether it’s chips or whether it’s doing manufacturing and packaging tests.”
Some 30 to 40 Chinese companies across the semiconductor supply chain were on track for initial public offerings (IPOs) on mainland exchanges this year, with three or four being key industry players, said Jimmy Yu, an analyst tracking the semiconductor sector at the Swiss Bank.
The abrupt ascent of Chinese AI start-up DeepSeek last year spurred a re-rating of Chinese technology stocks, and multiples got a further boost after investors identified the nation’s leading positions in areas from humanoid robotics to AI infrastructure. The frenzy led to a flurry of listings – domestically or in Hong Kong – by prominent AI companies including graphics processing unit (GPU) maker Moore Threads Technology and AI model start-up Zhipu AI.
The technology-dominated Star Market on the Shanghai exchange is currently valued at 74.1 times earnings, while the multiple for the tech start-ups on Shenzhen’s ChiNext board is 52.9 times, according to bourse data. The figure for the Nasdaq 100 is 38.1 times.
The Star Market includes China’s biggest chipmaker Semiconductor Manufacturing International and GPU maker Cambricon Technologies, which is seen as China’s Nvidia.
More technology IPOs will boost the representation of emerging strategic industries on the mainland’s exchanges, which are still dominated by companies in traditional, old-economy industries. In the US, market capitalisation is concentrated on the so-called Magnificent Seven technology companies, including Nvidia and Apple, that powered years of outperformance by US equities against other global markets.
A listing frenzy would be good for investors, as key AI players have all surged in their mainland debuts. Moore Threads jumped 425 per cent from its offer price on its first day of trading on December 5, and peer MetaX Integrated Circuits surged 693 per cent on December 17.
Swiss private bank Lombard Odier said technology would be a key investment theme for Chinese stocks this year, because of strong earnings growth and faster adoption of advanced technologies.
“China is closing the tech gap with the US and building spare power capacity to support its AI ambitions,” it said in a report this week. “Chinese equities are volatile, but sectors designated as strategic policy priorities can outperform.”

data.title: Hong Kong artist challenges disability labels in new exhibition
data.description:
Artist and disability justice advocate Sophie Cheung Hing-yee was diagnosed with schizophrenia when she was 13. By 25, she had stopped taking medication, but her doctor would not declare her “recovered”, only that her symptoms were in “remission”.
“That label … it felt like a wardrobe I could never step out of, a long, dark youth,” says Cheung, who is now 42.
It is more than a label. Under the Hong Kong Mental Health Ordinance, individuals like Cheung could still be considered “mentally incapable” even when they are not receiving treatment.
This legal concept, which dates back to the British colonial era, is the focus of her provocative new exhibition at Eaton HK, titled “Decolonising Madness: The Naturally (In)complete Human”.

According to the ordinance, which was introduced in 1962, “mental incapacity” is applied to those suffering from a condition that renders them “incapable of managing and administering their property and affairs; or persons suffering from mental illness or psychopathic disorders or having significantly impaired intelligence”.
Cheung is best known for her ongoing “Erasing News” series, which involves transferring newsprint to erasers to challenge perceptions of irreconcilable contradictions and to respond to dramatic changes in Hong Kong society.
There are examples from that series in her new show, but most pieces are collaborative works Cheung has made with participants in her community art workshops in Hong Kong and London over the last decade. Articles and poems by writers such as Shiu Ka-chun, the late social worker turned opposition lawmaker, also feature.
In one piece called What are the Qualifications to be a Person with Psychosocial Disability in Hong Kong?, a CV template asks viewers to think about which “traits or skills” are necessary to “qualify” as a mentally incapacitated person. The answers, which range from a doctor’s certificate to fulfilling common stereotypes, are presented on a wall display with dried tangerine peel, or chun pei in Cantonese, attached.

“Chun pei does not rot,” Cheung says, referring to the ingredient that is prized in Chinese cooking and traditional medicine. “It becomes more fragrant over time. Can our experiences, even stigmatised ones, become like that – precious and aromatic?”
This theme of looking at health holistically in contrast to Western legal and medical frameworks also appears in Crystal-Pills (2025), a sculpture Cheung made with pills to look like a natural crystal. It is shown with texts related to the Law Reform Commission of Hong Kong’s 1997 findings on the concept of “mental incapacity”.
“These pills are a temporary intervention,” Cheung says. “But here, they become a crystal – a form that [can take] over a millennium to grow.”
The work asks whether the “health” defined by prescriptions and routine is a fleeting solution, contrasting it with slow, geological processes.

Cheung says she wants to highlight the disempowering impact of diagnoses related to “mental incapacity”; they strip away individuals’ agency and dignity and subject them to systems of guardianship. She also considers the rigid categorisation of mental health as a legacy of the colonial habit of pathologising natural human differences.
“In a world that labels systemic failures as ‘madness’, there are orders and laws that classify and assess who is a ‘complete’ person,” she says.
“But if we are all part of nature, then every life is, by its very essence, the same: naturally incomplete. Trees do not judge each other ‘disabled’ – they coexist, sharing nutrients in unconditional symbiosis.”
Her philosophy is shaped by the concept of “dis-identification”, she says – a strategy of neither fully accepting nor rejecting labels like “disability” or “recovery”, but reclaiming and reworking them.
For Cheung, art is a way to promote a more fluid understanding of the world, and “to dissolve the permanence of labels”.

“Art gave me a sense of freedom and a voice. It’s not about whether a condition is an illness, but about who has the power to define it as such,” she says.
As part of “Decolonising Madness”, on January 17, Cheung will hold a public conversation with scholars and cultural workers Chow Yiu-fai, Po-han Lee and Shiou-wu Wang to further dissect the intersections of law, disability justice and art.
“Decolonising Madness: The Naturally (In)complete Human”, Tomorrow Maybe, 4/F, Eaton HK, 380 Nathan Road, Jordan, Hong Kong, January 9 to February 1, Mondays to Sundays, 11am-9pm.

data.title: Philippine minister’s ‘premature’ Myanmar visit casts doubts over Asean’s role
data.description:
A visit by the Philippines’ foreign minister to Myanmar has raised concerns over how Asean will be perceived under Manila’s chairmanship this year, with critics arguing it risks appearing as tacit support for the junta amid an election widely condemned as a sham.
Foreign Secretary Theresa Lazaro met Senior General Min Aung Hlaing and other junta leaders in Naypyidaw on January 6 in her capacity as special envoy from the Association of Southeast Asian Nations’ 2026 chair, according to official statements from both sides.
She was appointed to the role in November by Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr to “facilitate a peaceful solution to the Myanmar crisis” after Malaysia had symbolically passed the torch to the Philippines at October’s 47th Asean summit in Kuala Lumpur.
Lazaro reportedly led a delegation to Naypyidaw that held introductory meetings with Min Aung Hlaing, the junta’s prime minister, Lieutenant-General Nyo Saw, and its foreign minister, Than Swe.
Her department said the talks were “warm and constructive”, covering regional security, Myanmar’s internal political situation and the implementation of Asean’s Five-Point Consensus – the 2021 peace plan calling for a halt to violence and dialogue among all parties.

Myanmar’s state media presented the talks as upbeat, saying the junta had briefed Lazaro on its continued efforts “to restore peace and stability”, including what it called the “successful convening” of the first phase of a multiparty election process, and its campaigns against transnational crime and online fraud.
The statement added that Lazaro thanked Myanmar for its cooperation and pledged to maintain engagement under the Philippines’ chairmanship.
A ‘legitimate’ junta?
Some analysts expressed concern that Lazaro’s visit would send the message that the Philippines was abandoning its pro-democracy position and Asean was tacitly accepting junta rule in Myanmar.
The junta, which seized power in a 2021 coup, controls less than half the country. The ensuing civil war has left more than 7,000 people dead, 30,000 imprisoned and hundreds of thousands displaced.

Hunter Marston, an adjunct fellow at the Centre for Strategic and International Studies’ Southeast Asia programme, wrote in a recent social media post that Lazaro was “the latest to be seen shaking hands” with Min Aung Hlaing and other top junta officials in Naypyidaw.
“For those who expected the Philippines to continue with its principled stance on Myanmar during its chairmanship of Asean, this is a worrying sign of the region’s willingness to overlook the junta’s atrocities and normalise military rule in Myanmar,” he added.
Other observers, however, defended Lazaro’s visit, saying she was acting in her role as the Philippines’ special envoy.
“People can disagree with how the Philippines approached this, but it is a valid exercise of the role,” said Julio Amador, the interim president of the Foundation for the National Interest and founder and trustee of the non-profit policy advisory firm FACTS Asia.
Amador said Lazaro’s trip was also in line with Asean’s Five-Point Consensus, adding that the Philippines was dealing with expectations to hold talks with various stakeholders as the bloc’s chair, following similar engagements by Malaysia with pro-democracy organisations from Myanmar.

Regarding online scams and human trafficking in Myanmar as an agenda item during Lazaro’s meetings in the country, Amador said the crisis had grown into a serious challenge affecting Asean.
Joanne Lin, a senior fellow and coordinator at the ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute’s Asean Studies Centre, said that it was “not unusual” for special envoys of presiding Asean chairs to engage the military leadership.
However, the visit had raised concerns over how Asean was perceived as Lazaro’s visit coincided with Myanmar’s controversial election process, Lin added.
The meeting came “at a moment when Asean has been explicit that elections should be preceded by a cessation of violence and inclusive dialogue, and when Asean itself has chosen not to send observers,” she told This Week in Asia.
“Against this backdrop, a meeting with Min Aung Hlaing inevitably raises questions about consistency.”

Noting that Lazaro did not engage with the exiled National Unity Government and ethnic organisations in Myanmar, Lin said such an outreach would have cast her visit as “more balanced and credible”. Without this balance, the visit could be seen as “premature normalisation” of ties with Naypyidaw.
“If Asean is serious about contributing to conflict management, engagements will need to be broader and more balanced over time, including outreach to other democratic stakeholders. Without that, such visits risk being seen as reinforcing the status quo rather than helping to move Myanmar towards a more inclusive and sustainable political pathway.”
Nonetheless, Lazaro’s visit highlighted the Philippines’ commitment to maintaining access to the authorities in Myanmar and Asean’s influence as well as advancing the bloc’s Five-Point Consensus, according to analysts.
Amador said the visit proved that Manila was facilitating Asean’s road map to restore democracy in Naypyidaw, noting that it would likely be discussed in the coming foreign ministers’ retreat in Cebu this month.
“The Philippines has long been a supporter of democracy in Myanmar. That record has been consistent over the years,” he added.
